topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the cramped, dimly lit bar was thick with cheap beer and forced merriment. Liam stood near the back wall, his knuckles white where he gripped his empty glass. The opening chords of "Echo Chamber," his masterpiece, drifted through the speakers, flawlessly executed by a stranger. A cold, hard knot tightened in his chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching that had been building for months. He watched the performer, a slick-haired young man with an infuriatingly easy stage presence, move with an arrogance that grated on Liam's nerves. Every note struck felt like a deliberate mockery. Liam didn't move, just absorbed the sound, letting the dissonance of the performance settle deep into his core. When the song ended, the applause was deafening, and Liam finally pushed off the wall, his gaze locking onto the performer. His eyes were flat, devoid of warmth, and he walked toward the stage with a slow, deliberate pace, every step an accusation.

[story 2]

I was nursing a lukewarm coffee, the melody of my latest track playing on repeat through my headphones. Then, the sound bled through the wall—a rendition of my composition, twisted, stripped of its intended melancholy. It was done by someone else. A surge of heat, sharp and immediate, shot up my neck. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing in the quiet apartment. My hands began to tremble, a tremor that wasn't from caffeine withdrawal. I paced the worn rug, the silence amplifying the drumming in my ears. I needed to know who dared to take what was mine, who dared to dilute the purity of my creation. I snatched my guitar, the wood feeling suddenly heavy and charged. I didn't plan to simply walk away; I planned to make sure the next performance was a lesson in ownership.

[story 3]

The concert hall was vast, the velvet seats swallowing the low murmur of the crowd. When the familiar, haunting progression of my song began, I felt a sickening lurch in my stomach. It wasn't just that someone else was playing it; it was the way they interpreted the bridge—a cheap, saccharine alteration that felt like a deliberate smear. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I watched the singer with intense scrutiny, cataloging every subtle shift in posture, every misplaced breath. A simmering intensity began to bubble beneath my skin, a restless energy demanding release. I leaned forward, my eyes burning into the performer, a silent promise passing between us—a promise that the true artist would soon reclaim their territory.
